content.problemStatement:
**This is the easy version of the problem. The only difference is the constraint on AiA_iAi‚Äã ‚Äî in this version, 1‚â§Ai‚â§21 \leq A_i \leq 21‚â§Ai‚Äã‚â§2.**

An array BBB of length MMM is said to normal if its mean equals its median.

The mean of BBB is defined to be

B1+B2+‚Ä¶+BMM\frac{B_1 + B_2 + \ldots + B_M}{M}MB1‚Äã+B2‚Äã+‚Ä¶+BM‚Äã‚ÄãFor example, the mean of [1,2][1, 2][1,2] is 32=1.5\frac{3}{2} = 1.523‚Äã=1.5, and the mean of [2,2,3,1,3][2, 2, 3, 1, 3][2,2,3,1,3] is 115=2.2\frac{11}{5} = 2.2511‚Äã=2.2.

The median of BBB is defined to be the element at index ‚åàM2‚åâ\left\lceil \frac{M}{2} \right\rceil‚åà2M‚Äã‚åâ when the elements of BBB are **sorted in ascending order**.

That is, the median of an odd-length array is the middle element after sorting, and the median of an even-length array is the left of the two middle elements after sorting.

For example, the median of [2,1,2][2, 1, 2][2,1,2] is 222 (sorted order is [1,2,2][1, 2, 2][1,2,2]), and the median of [3,1,2,2,1,1][3, 1, 2, 2, 1, 1][3,1,2,2,1,1] is 111 (sorted order is [1,1,1,2,2,3][1, 1, 1, 2, 2, 3][1,1,1,2,2,3], take the left of the two middle elements which is 111.)

You're given an array AAA containing NNN integers, **each of which lies between 111 and 222.**

Count the number of contiguous subarrays of AAA that are normal.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN ‚Äî the length of the array AAA.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,‚Ä¶,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1‚Äã,A2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,AN‚Äã.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the number of normal subarrays of AAA.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 3‚ãÖ1053\cdot 10^53‚ãÖ105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Every subarray contains only twos, so the mean and median are always 222.
Every subarray is normal, so the answer is the count of subarrays, which is 666.
Test case 222: The only normal subarrays are those with length 111.
